---
title: "Comparing panel estimators with cfcompare"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    self_contained: true
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Comparing panel estimators with cfcompare}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  # This vignette uses PRECOMPUTED figures kept in man/figures/ -- the very same
  # images shown in the README and in the help pages. The estimator code below is
  # shown for reference but is NOT run at build time, which keeps R CMD build,
  # R CMD check and pkgdown fast (a full cross-validation + bootstrap per figure
  # would otherwise dominate every check). Copy any snippet into an R session to
  # run it live. Figures render because the output is self-contained: each
  # man/figures/*.png is embedded (base64) into the built HTML, so the vignette
  # still shows them after the package is installed.
  eval = FALSE,
  echo = TRUE
)
# Chunks that display a shared figure opt in with `opts.label = "fig"`.
knitr::opts_template$set(fig = list(
  eval = TRUE, echo = FALSE, out.width = "100%", fig.align = "center"
))
```

## The problem

You have a binary-treatment panel and several plausible estimators: classic
difference-in-differences (DID), synthetic difference-in-differences (SDID),
synthetic control (SC), matrix completion (MC), and the Triply RObust Panel
(TROP) estimator of Athey, Imbens, Qu & Viviano (2026). Each lives in its own
package with its own data format, its own notion of a "treatment effect" and its
own plot. Comparing them on the *same* dataset means re-shaping data five times
and reconciling five different output objects by hand.

`cfcompare` does that reconciliation for you. You pass one long-format data frame
and a vector of method names; you get back one tidy table of ATTs and one plot.

```{r setup}
library(cfcompare)
```

## A simulated panel

`sim_panel()` draws from a low-rank factor model
`Y_it(0) = alpha_i + beta_t + L_it + eps_it` and adds a constant treatment effect
to treated cells, so we know the ground truth.

```{r sim}
df <- sim_panel(N = 16, T = 9, n_treated = 3, t0 = 7, rank = 2,
                att = 2, noise = 1, seed = 1)
head(df)
```

The columns are the unit id (`id`), time (`t`), observed outcome (`y`), the 0/1
treatment indicator (`w`), and the oracle untreated outcome (`y0`, for
evaluation only). The true ATT here is `2`.

## Comparing estimators

```{r compare}
cmp <- panel_compare(
  df,
  outcome = "y", treatment = "w", unit = "id", time = "t",
  methods = c("DID", "MC", "TROP"),
  se = "jackknife",
  control = trop_control(n_cv_cells = 12L, cv_cycles = 1L)
)
cmp
```

The `att` element is a `cf_att_tbl`: one row per method, all on the same schema
(`cmp$att`). If you omit `methods`, `panel_compare()` runs its default set ---
`TROP`, `DID`, `SC`, `MC`, `SDID`, and `DIFP` (the native engines plus synthetic
DID and synthetic control). To run the defaults *without* a particular method,
pass `exclude` rather than retyping the list, e.g.
`panel_compare(..., exclude = "DIFP")`. The same `methods` / `exclude` arguments
work in `panel_rmse()` and `rmse_curve()`.

Every estimate carries the number of treated cells and units, the estimated rank
of the low-rank component `L` (for the native engines), and a `note` column that
records anything that was skipped.

`autoplot()` on the comparison gives a forest plot of the ATTs with their
confidence intervals:

```{r forest-code, eval = FALSE}
autoplot(cmp)
```

```{r forest-fig, opts.label = "fig"}
knitr::include_graphics("../man/figures/compare_forest.png")
```

`plot_counterfactual()` overlays the observed treated-average path against each
estimator's predicted untreated path, so you can see *where* the methods
disagree, not just by how much:

```{r cf, eval = FALSE}
plot_counterfactual(cmp)
```

## One estimator at a time

`trop()` is the workhorse. Called on its own it cross-validates the three
penalties --- time-weight decay, unit-weight decay, and the nuclear-norm penalty
on `L` --- by leaving out control cells one at a time and scoring the prediction.

```{r trop-fit}
fit <- trop(df, "y", "w", "id", "t", se = "jackknife",
            control = trop_control(n_cv_cells = 12L, cv_cycles = 1L))
fit
```

The fitted object exposes the per-cell effects, the estimated untreated-outcome
matrix, and the selected penalties:

```{r trop-internals}
head(fit$tau_cells)
fit$lambda
```

Calling `autoplot()` on a single `trop` fit gives a synthetic-control-style
trajectory: the treated-unit average against the estimated `Y(0)`, with a dashed
line marking the first treated period (the post-treatment gap between the two
lines is the effect, left unshaded so it is not mistaken for a confidence band).
Because TROP carries explicit time weights, those weights are drawn as a filled
band along the bottom, in the style of `synthdid`'s time-weight strip. The band
shows *time weights* --- how much each period the counterfactual leans on --- not
observation counts; it is flat when `lambda_time = 0` (uniform weighting). Use
`show_weights = FALSE` to drop the band.

This trajectory shows only point estimates --- no standard errors --- so by
default the subtitle reports just the selected penalties (set `show_se = TRUE` to
also note the SE method). To *see* the fit's uncertainty, use `trop_event_study()`
(next section). The `se =` argument on `trop()` still controls the standard error
shown in `print()`, `as_att()`, and the comparison forest plot.

```{r trop-autoplot, eval = FALSE}
autoplot(fit)
```

```{r trajectory-fig, opts.label = "fig"}
knitr::include_graphics("../man/figures/trop_trajectory.png")
```

### Per-period effects (event study)

`trop_event_study()` breaks the single ATT into per-period effects indexed by
event time (periods relative to each unit's first treated period). It reuses the
fit's resampling --- aggregating each resample's per-cell effects *by event time*
rather than into one mean --- so it adds no modelling assumptions beyond those of
the fit itself. With `pre_periods = TRUE` (the default) the pre-treatment gaps
come back as placebo / pre-trend points, and `autoplot()` draws the event-study
figure: per-period points with pointwise confidence bars, pre-treatment points
shown distinctly, a dotted line at treatment onset, and a solid line at zero. A
flat pre-period profile and a clear post-treatment jump are the visual signature
of a credible design. The per-period intervals are wider than the overall ATT
interval and are pointwise rather than simultaneous.

```{r trop-event}
es <- trop_event_study(fit, se = "bootstrap")   # bootstrap is the default
es
```

```{r es-code, eval = FALSE}
autoplot(es)
```

```{r es-fig, opts.label = "fig"}
knitr::include_graphics("../man/figures/trop_event_study_bootstrap.png")
```

### Is the effect distinguishable from zero? A placebo test

`trop_placebo_test()` asks a different question from the standard errors above:
*could an effect this large appear by chance if the treatment did nothing?* It
takes a fitted `trop` object, repeatedly re-assigns the treated units' timing
pattern to randomly chosen **control** units (where the true effect is zero),
re-estimates the ATT at the fit's selected penalties, and builds the resulting
**placebo null distribution**. The observed ATT is then compared against that
null; the share of placebo draws as extreme as the observed effect is the
`p.value` (`alternative` chooses a one- or two-sided test).

```{r placebo-test}
pt <- trop_placebo_test(fit, B = 500)
pt
```

`autoplot()` draws the null as a histogram with the observed ATT marked, so it is
easy to see whether the real estimate sits in the tail:

```{r pt-code, eval = FALSE}
autoplot(pt)
```

```{r pt-fig, opts.label = "fig"}
knitr::include_graphics("../man/figures/placebo_test.png")
```

This randomization test is the recommended tool for the "is it distinguishable
from zero" question; use the bootstrap or jackknife (below) for a standard error
and confidence interval on the effect size itself.

### Comparing standard-error methods

The point estimate is fixed by cross-validation; only the *interval* depends on
how you resample. `compare_se_modes()` refits the same estimate under each SE
method so you can see how the intervals differ:

```{r se-modes}
se_cmp <- compare_se_modes(
  df, "y", "w", "id", "t",
  se = c("bootstrap", "jackknife"),
  control = trop_control(n_cv_cells = 12L, cv_cycles = 1L)
)
se_cmp
```

`autoplot()` shows one interval per method with a common estimate. Zero is always
drawn (a dashed reference line) and kept in view, because for inference what
matters is whether the interval clears zero:

```{r se-code, eval = FALSE}
autoplot(se_cmp)
```

```{r se-fig, opts.label = "fig"}
knitr::include_graphics("../man/figures/se_comparison.png")
```

### Recovering special cases

The TROP program nests DID and matrix completion. Fixing the penalties by hand
matches them numerically, which is a useful sanity check:

```{r special}
did <- trop(df, "y", "w", "id", "t",
            lambda = list(time = 0, unit = 0, nn = Inf))
mc  <- trop(df, "y", "w", "id", "t",
            lambda = list(time = 0, unit = 0, nn = 5))
c(DID = did$att, MC = mc$att, TROP = fit$att)
```

With `lambda_nn = Inf` and uniform weights the low-rank term vanishes and the fit
is ordinary two-way fixed effects; with a finite `lambda_nn` and uniform weights
it is matrix completion; letting cross-validation pick the unit and time weights
gives the full TROP estimator. Passing an explicit `lambda` also skips
cross-validation entirely --- the way to line cfcompare up against another TROP
implementation on identical penalties.

### Penalty ablation

`trop_ablation()` runs that whole sweep of constraints for you --- full TROP,
then dropping the unit weights, the time weights, the regression adjustment, and
the combinations that reduce to matrix completion and difference-in-differences.
With `rmse = TRUE` it scores each specification by placebo RMSE relative to full
TROP (the robustness exercise behind the paper's Table 5, `TROP = 1.00`):

```{r ablation}
ab <- trop_ablation(df, "y", "w", "id", "t",
                    rmse = TRUE, horizon = 3, n_pseudo = 5, n_runs = 100,
                    control = trop_control(n_cv_cells = 12L, cv_cycles = 1L))
ab
```

`format()` renders it as paste-ready LaTeX (booktabs) or Markdown, `plot()` draws
the paper-style table as a figure, and passing `se = "jackknife"` (or any [trop()]
SE method) adds standard-error and confidence-interval columns. Because the result
is an ordinary `data.frame`, it also drops straight into `knitr::kable()`,
`gt::gt()`, and similar.

```{r ablation-fmt, eval = FALSE}
cat(format(ab, "latex"), sep = "\n")     # needs \usepackage{booktabs}
writeLines(format(ab, "markdown"))
plot(ab)                                 # paper-style Table-5 figure
```

```{r ablation-fig, opts.label = "fig"}
knitr::include_graphics("../man/figures/trop_ablation.png")
```

### Adding covariates

When covariates help explain the untreated outcome, the low-rank term can be
split additively as `L_js = X_js·phi + R_js` (Section 6.2): the covariate-linear
part is unpenalised and the nuclear norm applies to the residual `R`. Pass the
column names through `covariates`; cross-validation is then run on the
covariate-residualised model, and the coefficients come back in `$phi`.

```{r covariates}
set.seed(1)
dfx <- df
dfx$x1 <- rnorm(nrow(dfx))
dfx$y  <- dfx$y + 1.3 * dfx$x1          # covariate is part of Y(0)
fitx <- trop(dfx, "y", "w", "id", "t", covariates = "x1", se = "none",
             control = trop_control(n_cv_cells = 12L, cv_cycles = 1L))
fitx$phi
```

Covariates must be fully observed (including treated cells) and flow through the
event study, the standard errors, and the plotted counterfactual automatically.

### A common counterfactual across methods

`counterfactual_matrix()` (alias `predict_counterfactual()`) returns any fit's
estimated `Y(0)` as an `N x T` matrix, so methods can be compared on the same
grid:

```{r cf-matrix}
M <- counterfactual_matrix(fit)
dim(M)
```

For native engines every cell is filled; methods that only define a treated-unit
counterfactual fill the treated rows and leave controls `NA`. This common
interface is what lets `panel_rmse(metric = "prediction")` score the paper's
Table-1 unit-period prediction error across DID, MC, TROP and DIFP (and SDID/SC
when `synthdid` is installed); `metric = "placebo"` scores placebo RMSE instead.

```{r pred-rmse, eval = FALSE}
panel_rmse(df, "y", "w", "id", "t",
           methods = c("DID", "MC", "TROP", "DIFP"),
           metric = "prediction", horizon = 3, n_pseudo = 5, n_runs = 5, seed = 1)
```

```{r rmse-fig, opts.label = "fig"}
knitr::include_graphics("../man/figures/rmse_prediction.png")
```

## Bringing your own results

If you already ran an estimator elsewhere --- say `synthdid` directly --- coerce
it onto the shared schema with `as_att()` and combine it with native fits:

```{r byo, eval = FALSE}
library(synthdid)
sd <- synthdid_estimate(Y, N0, T0)

combined <- as_att(list(
  fit,
  as_att(sd, method = "SDID", outcome = "y")
))
autoplot(combined)
```

## Designs and inference

The native engines (DID, MC, TROP) accept **block**, **staggered**, and
**non-absorbing** binary treatments: `treatment` is simply the 0/1 indicator of
active treatment in each cell. `SDID` and `SC` are defined for block designs only
and are skipped, with a note, on other designs.

By default the native engines report a **bootstrap** standard error: a
unit-level stratified block bootstrap (resampling treated and control units
separately, with replacement, keeping each unit's full time series), the approach
used for TROP's standard errors and confidence intervals in the paper. Percentile
intervals are the default; tune the bootstrap with
`trop_control(n_boot =, boot_ci =)`. The alternative is **jackknife**
(leave-one-treated-unit-out; it needs at least two treated units), available via
`se = "jackknife"`. `se = "auto"` picks jackknife when there are at least two
treated units and falls back to the bootstrap otherwise. These are practical
approximations; see Section 6 of the paper for the estimator's formal inference
theory. For "is the effect distinguishable from zero" as a hypothesis, use
`trop_placebo_test()` (above) rather than a standard error.

## Performance

The native solver runs on base R by default, but two settings speed it up on
larger panels (both via `trop_control()`):

* **`svd`** --- the soft-impute step uses a **truncated SVD by default**
  (`svd = "truncated"`), computing only the leading singular triplets with
  `RSpectra` when it is installed (and falling back to the full base-R `svd()`
  otherwise). The TROP paper explicitly permits a truncated decomposition for the
  low-rank step, and it matches the full SVD to numerical tolerance. Pass
  `svd = "full"` to force the exact full decomposition; that is what the README's
  numerical-agreement checks against the official Python package use, so the
  comparison is exact rather than up to truncation tolerance.
* **`workers`** --- the bootstrap and jackknife standard errors, the
  cross-validation cells, and the `panel_rmse()` placebo runs are run in parallel
  when `workers > 1` (using `future.apply` / `future`, if installed). Estimates
  remain reproducible given `seed`.

```{r perf, eval = FALSE}
trop(df, "y", "w", "id", "t", se = "bootstrap",
     control = trop_control(svd = "truncated", workers = 4))
```

## Reference

Athey, S., Imbens, G. W., Qu, Z., & Viviano, D. (2026). *Triply Robust Panel
Estimators.* Journal of Applied Econometrics.
<https://doi.org/10.1002/jae.70061> (working paper: arXiv:2508.21536).
